speaker: interviewer
text: March the 14th, 1996, in London, England. Bertha, would you introduce your family please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd love to. It's a shame I haven't got the whole family to show you. This is my daughter, my older daughter Shula, Shula Kohn. And this is her middle daughter, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hadassa, [? Dassie. ?] And these are [? Dassie's ?] children, my grand-- my great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Great grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's your name? Can you say your name, Michalle? Michalle Forta. Michalle Forta. Say and I'm two years old. And this one is Jonathan, Jonathan. [? Joni, ?] We call him [? Joni ?] for short-- Forta. And he is exactly three months old. And there is one that's missing. She lives in-- he lives in Switzerland with his mummy, who is Rachel, who is her sister, a year older than [? Dassie, ?] and with her daddy, who is Chaim. And they're called Margulis now. And Perhaps we'll show you a photograph later. Because I must show the whole family. And apart from that, Shula's still got--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yakov--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who else have you got?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Who's 21.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yakov is 21. He's just arrived from Israel. He goes to Yeshiva there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Esti--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Esti who is 17, and she is in the seminar in Manchester. They're a very orthodox family, as are the other branch of the family and as I. I'm pleased to say most of our families are, cousins included, all around. So-- so later on, we'll show you a photograph. Come on, Michalle, come on over here. You won't be in the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go to mummy. Go to mummy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll carry him for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Come on, come around here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll lift you over. Want me to lift you to mummy? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Come on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go to mummy, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. Lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came specially to say hello. So they'll be in the picture. And in fact, [? Dassie ?] is the one who was exactly the same age as I was, who gave me the idea-- as I was, when I came to England. And she was the one whose photograph gave me the idea of starting the reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think of your grandmother's work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm very proud of her. I really am. It's incredible what she's done. And brought people back to the religion as well. It's fantastic, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot of people are turning back because they haven't been in touch with Judaism. Even if they're not religious, it doesn't matter. But they know that they're Jews again.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're finding their roots.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they're finding their roots again. Yes, and that is important.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm very, very proud of my mother, for what she's done for the Kindertransport. And I wish her many, many healthy years to do more work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Came all the way from [INAUDIBLE], didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not far, not so far.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that far.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry my other children couldn't be here. That's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we'll show them a photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bertha, is there anything else you'd like to say to your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing that I want to say most--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the children that aren't here, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --That never, ever should the thing arise, the-- such a dreadful decision-making arise, that they would have to send their little children away. And they can't understand--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when you asked me. Do you remember? How, how would you feel.

speaker: interviewee
text: How would you have felt. In that talk in the library, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: How would you feel if you had to send away your little girl. I had my little girl. And I thought to myself, oh my goodness, I can't even imagine what it must be like to, to have to send away your own child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even little babies like that. The youngest one was not-- the same age as this one now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you going to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you going to say hello? Hello, sweetheart. Hello.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the years to come, I'm going to get a copy of this film, aren't I? So in the years to come, we will show him, yes. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: For your patience. And thanks to the cameraman, for his patience and hard work. And thank you for coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bertha where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very old photograph. It's one of the oldest I have. That was taken 1922, actually at my parents-- a few days after my parents' wedding. The old couple in the center are my grandparents, who came specially from Poland to Germany for the occasion. And next to the grandmother, sits my mother, the young bride. And behind her with this arm around her shoulder, is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the gentleman with the stripey tie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with the stripey tie. That's my father. He looks like Charlie Chaplin there. Next to him, is his-- it's all my father's side of the family. Next to him is his brother, [? Belo. ?] I remember he went later on to, to France. As my parents went to Germany, they went to France. Next to-- oh, next to my father on the right-hand side is my cousin [? Mali ?] who is an old lady now in Israel. And in front of her sits-- next to my mother, is my cousin [? Annie, ?] who is just about three years younger than my mother. And she became my in-law because her youngest son and my youngest daughter in Israel got married. They met in Israel, and they got married. That's her youngest son and my youngest daughter. But the age difference, she's nearly as old as my mother. Next--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's the woman left of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Left to her is her mother. That was Tante Feigele. And behind her is her husband, Uncle [? Leibish. ?] And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that is [? Mali. ?] Next to this one, we've identified already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to him his Uncle-- I believe that's Uncle [? Meyer. ?] Who are we looking at here, Bertha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to the gentleman with the hat is Uncle [? Meyer, ?] also lived in Munich. And in front of him, the lady with the fur collar-- she was always very posh-- is [? Tante ?] [? Fannie. ?] And next to her are-- behind them, I think that is another sister. That is another-- wait a minute, which, no, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's behind the boy with the sailor collar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Behind the boy in the sailor collar-- I'm sorry, I've made a mistake. This is not Tante [? Fannie. ?] That is one of the cousins. This is Tante [? Fannie ?] on the right-hand side. The boy who's got his hand on her shoulder, that is his-- that this his mother. And behind her, behind her, on the-- on the last, the last one on the photograph, that's Uncle [? Meyer. ?] And these two other, the one behind him I think he's called, he was called [? Johannes. ?] He's one of the family of this lady next, on the right of him. Is, I think that is, that was the sister-in-law, that's right. That is one of the sister-in-laws of my-- wives of my uncle. Now, in front of me, oh those two boys! They were my favorite cousins. Those are the ones I talked about. They lived in Birmingham. That's on the right-hand is-- no, on the right-hand, with short cut hair-- with the fringe-- that's [? Noli. ?] We called him [? Nobby ?] later on. He died, unfortunately, here in England. And his older brother, Hertz, Hertzi, he was my favorite. He's exactly 10 years older than me. Wait a minute. He's 12 years older than me. [? Nobby's ?] 10 years. Because I wasn't born then. And all the other cousins in front. This is [? Beno. ?] He lives in Israel now. And those are [? Mali ?] and [NON-ENGLISH]. Those are two cousins of mine in-- they live in Israel as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is an adopted sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's on the-- that's a, that's, that's-- [? Bimi ?] and--

speaker: interviewer
text: The one with the-- and they've both got bows. The one on the right is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is [? Bimi, ?] and that is [? Regi. ?] [? Regi, ?] she lives on Kibbutz [NON-ENGLISH]. She's one of the-- her husband was one of the founder members of Kibbutz [NON-ENGLISH]. And when we see-- when they make a, a family party, you've never seen anything like it. Because thank god there must be-- they're all orthodox, and they've all got lots of children and grandchildren and great-grandchildren. And when there's a family get together, I know all the first cousins. And they all introduce their children to me. I know their children, but not their grandchildren. So there are about 500 to 600 of us for a family wedding. [INAUDIBLE]. And not only that, because I'm inter-related to them, because my son-in-law in Israel, my husband's-- my, my-- my younger daughter's husband, he is the son. He's their cousin. He's their second cousin as well. He's the son of my cousin Annie, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the family gets together-- It's quite an occasion.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is quite an occasion. And this cousin is called [? Rosul. ?] She was adopted by their parents from, from Poland. She was brought over from Poland. She became an orphan at an early age. But she shared in everything in all the family. She-- I never remember a time when there wasn't [? Rosul. ?] And the funniest thing is with the lady sitting next to my mother, Annie, I was her bridesmaid together with [? Bobby. ?] No, [? Bobby ?] wasn't born then either, because she's six months younger than me. So we were her bridesmaids. And later on, she became my in-law, which is quite an amazing story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that is the family photograph of my father's side. On my mother's side, I haven't got old photographs. They seem to have died much younger. My father had a very long living family. And this, I-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is pertaining to the photograph you've just seen, from my grandfather from Poland. He was a scribe, as you can see by the beautiful handwriting. That was their golden wedding, in 1926. And he wrote that. He made something like that for every branch of the family, very beautiful. He was very, very talented. Now, this is my first birthday photograph, with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the photo-- it was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, well, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich, yes. Save you time. [LAUGHTER] This is the photograph of my best friend in Germany. Her name was Rosi Kohn, and she is Hungarian. And unfortunately, just a few weeks before the war ended, her father told me later-- he was still alive, the only member of the family-- they went to Budapest back again. And she was taken away and never heard of again. And her brother, who did the beautiful writing on the right-hand side, Manfred Kohn-- he was in my class, and she was a year older. And she gave me this book, which I've kept and treasured ever since I left Germany. That was in 19-- she, she gave it to me in 1938. And I left beginning of January '39. And I would like to commemorate her, because she has nobody who is left to mention her name even. Her name was Rosi Kohn and [? Manfred ?] Kohn and her parents, who died in Hungary. Where she died, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this, Bertha?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means 'figures of world history through four year hundreds.' It is written in German, and it contains miniatures-- reproduction of miniatures, actual miniatures-- which were painted. It tells you the name of the painter on the back, Durer, all the most famous painters. And they were given by the kings and princes to people whom they wanted to honor. And they all true, as they were painted then. And some of them must have got lost, now. That's what makes the book valuable.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the book you had with you to the shops.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the book I had--yes, yes, I rescued that. The other one they took, the one of the animals. On the back of each picture, it tells you the name of the painter, when it was painted, the subject matter. And the value of the book, it comes in because so many-- these are all original-- were original miniatures. And a lot of them must have got lost over the years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here I've got what they looked like, if you want to just turn a page to just approximately, you see? Those were actual miniatures. And the color, actually, for those days, is pretty good. Because there weren't any color photographs. That book is 1937, I believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph which I love very much. I never met my grandmother, the one who came from Poland to Jerusalem. This is her. She was almost 100 years old on the photograph, with her oldest son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Rische Engelhard. She came from Poland, from Prochnik. And her eldest son is on the right-hand side. And that is Uncle Yudel. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which, which is the one-- who's the older gentleman, sitting down?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Uncle Yudel, who was-- and this his Uncle [? Meyer, ?] a younger son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uncle [? Meyer's ?] on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm sorry because my father went to visit her as well, twice, from England. In those days, you didn't run around so easily to Israel as you do now. And because, I mean, at 100 years old, you have to go and visit her. And that is his mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That just taken I would think shortly after the war, about 1950, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- these, these are my parents, Moshe and Rachel, Rosa Engelhard. And you see how happy they look? Because it is the bar mitzvah photograph of my son, of my son, Danny. And that was taken in Birmingham, when he was bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born 1946. And that would be '59, wouldn't it? 1959, yes. And they look so young and happy there, much nicer than when they came over to England first. That was my son Danny. That was-- he was already rather ill then. And he was 20 years old on that photograph. He was a very special boy. And it was just very, very sad what happened. He died in-- actually he died in London. And he's buried in London, because we tried one last thing to bring him over here. He died in my sister's house. He did not die in hospital. That is my brother, my late brother Theodor Engelhard. And sadly, he died on his 43rd birthday in London. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He left three boys. And none of them are married yet. So it's rather sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph can't have been taken long before he died. He might have been about 41, 42, on that photograph. This is my lovely family in Israel. I'm very proud of them. It's already about three years old now because the baby you can see is now four. And Danny now is a soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is Danny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danny's behind the baby on the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left-hand side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, on the left-hand side. He's 21 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the baby's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby's name is Uriel. And this is Daniel, not Danny. His Name is Daniel. And the one in front of him is his little sister, Oshra.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the one wearing tartan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, tartan. And she's supposed to look very much like me or having looked like me when I was a baby. And next to her--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wearing white?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wearing white is Avital, Avital who is now 11, and I think she looks more like her mother. I'm not quite sure. And this is my daughter, [? Miri, ?] who is my younger daughter, born 1952. And she is very, very talented. She's a great artist. She has just now made-- designed and made herself 17 huge glass stained windows for the new synagogue in Kedumim, where they live on the West Bank in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Alon. He is now-- Which is Alon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alon, the little boy in the, in the colorful jumper. He is now 15. He's a very bright-- he's not such a little boy anymore. He goes to Yeshiva now. And behind him is my lovely son-in-law, who is also I believe my second cousin. That's right, he's my second cousin, too. He's the youngest son of my cousin [? Mali, ?] who lived in Israel. So his-- we share one great-grandfather, that is the thing, one great-grandfather. And next to him is my lovely granddaughter, Orit, which means light. And she is now 18. And she is doing-- instead of national service, she is, because the orthodox girl don't go into national service-- she does voluntary work. She works with handicapped children. And she does-- she will be doing that for two or three years. And she works very hard. She's a very nice, special girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph was taken-- by the baby, I can tell about three years ago. I haven't got a more recent one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which would be 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I know when it was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was taken for my-- I complained. I said, I haven't got any photographs, and that was taken for my 70th birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In January, 19-- what are we now? Three years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1993, that's right. Yes. The boy is my grandson Yakov, who should have been here today but couldn't quite make it. And those are his sisters. Behind him, the dark one is called Rachel, who now lives in Switzerland and has got a lovely little boy called Tuvi. I must get his photograph in. He is just a year old, a couple of weeks ago. And in front of her, the blonde one is [? Dassie ?] whom you've seen, who came here to, to say hello, and whose children you've seen-- little girl and a little boy. And the next one to her who's now 17, she is in seminar in Manchester. Her name is Esther. We call her Esti.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the photograph was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: That photograph must be already about three or four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be four years old, because [? Dassie's ?] been married already. Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I just wanted to show it. Because I haven't got a photograph of-- a more recent photograph. I have got one somewhere of Yakov. And I can see now, my friends used to say he looks very much like Danny. I can only just begin to see the likeness. I didn't want to see it before. There was only one Danny for me. And even my grandchildren, nothing, no one can replace him. The little girl is the one who you've just seen. That is Hadassa, Hadassa Forta's little girl Michalle. You saw her before. But I wanted to show it to you because this is my little other little great-grandson. He was a year old two weeks ago in Switzerland when I visited them. His mother is Rachel Margulis, married to Chaim Margulis. And that is their little boy called Tuvi, who has his grandfather's name, my first husband's name, Tuvie. This is a recent photograph of my sister in Israel called Inge Malka Sadan. She's also very, very handy and very clever. And she makes those lovely terrariums, which you can see in front of her. They are very special. She can do anything. I'm not handy at all. And that is a recent photograph of her, Inge.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1995-- yes, yes. Last year's, yes. She's seven years and one day younger than me. Although it has a sad content-- they're gravestones-- it is a very happy picture, which not many of my friends are in possession of such a wonderful picture. Those are the gravestones of my parents next to each other, buried here in London-- in Bushey. And although it's sad, my mother died at the age of 73, 74, which is still quite young. My father lived to the age of 88. And at least I've got their graves to visit and also my brother's grave. My brother's buried there. And my Danny's somewhere else but also in London, buried in London. And I think it's important to realize how, how happy I am to have a grave to visit of my parents. This is the book called And you can see it has won a literary prize for nonfiction, which I didn't even put it in for. It is the collective reminiscences of about 250 kinder who sent me their reports. It's a historical book, because it shows their names, former names, town of origin, and town of domicile now, worldwide, above their stories, which are run in alphabetical order. The little girl on the left is from one of our archives. But the child on the right is the entry visa with my little sister's picture on it. And she was nine years old. You can see when she arrived in England. I think it was the 12th of July, 21st of July, 1939. And that is her entry visa to Great Britain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why we used it. Because it is a good picture. And that was the reason we used it on the front page.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, the same-- a similar page is on our brochure, which is so important worldwide, which shows all the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] names, which contains all the names, former names, and full addresses of hundreds and hundreds of members of our group. And this is unique, because worldwide they can get in touch with one another. Hundreds and hundreds of people. I've got so many letters. I could produce another book, just of the letters saying thank you for having found a friend through this reunion. And I must mention my friend, the late Shmuel Lowensohn. He compiled this reunion. It was a tremendous lot of work. And very, very sadly, he died. And he was also my co-editor for the book, Last year, my sister helped in producing a wonderful book called The Jews-- the [POLISH], the Polish-origin Jews-- in Munich. And there were found archives, in Munich itself, that we have never ever seen before. This one, this page, is pertaining to my mother, Rosa Engelhard. You can see her signature. My father was away in, in the concentration camp. He was sent home later on. And she had to sign every week. And the important thing is that the signature stopped after-- the last date was the 23rd of the 8th, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they lived in Munich til that date. And after that, they escaped. So this gives us the date of their escape from Germany, a year into the war, which is something quite unique.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was found in the actual German archives. We have never seen that before. So it shows how, how precise the Germans are. They kept all the records. That's how we know so much about what has happened there. And this is a point in fact of my mother's signature so many years ago. This photograph, I, I really am quite ashamed to tell people, I was only 15 then. I was quite a sophisticated 15-year-old, going on 16 of course. And just before my journey to England. That was me, just at the age before I left Germany for England, nearly 16.
